terms.def: (Return − r_f) / σ — risk-adjusted return per unit of total volatility; higher = better.
The baseline return requiring no risk; subtracted so only compensated risk-taking is measured.
Measures total return volatility; denominator of Sharpe; captures both upside and downside fluctuations.
Like Sharpe but divides by downside deviation only — penalises only harmful volatility, not upside.
CAGR / Max Drawdown — an alternative risk-adjusted metric popular in alternative investments.
Active return / tracking error — the Sharpe ratio of active management relative to a benchmark.

eli3: The Sharpe ratio measures how much return you get for each unit of risk you take. It's calculated as: (return − risk-free rate) / standard deviation. A higher Sharpe ratio means you're getting more return per unit of risk — better risk-adjusted performance.

If two investments have the same return, the one with lower volatility has a higher Sharpe ratio — it's a better deal. If two investments have the same volatility, the one with higher return has a higher Sharpe ratio. The risk-free rate (usually a short-term Treasury yield) is subtracted because risk-free return requires no skill — you're only measuring the return you earned above what you could have gotten without taking any risk.

The Sharpe ratio was developed by William Sharpe in 1966 (originally called the 'reward-to-variability ratio') and is the most widely used risk-adjusted performance metric in finance. A Sharpe ratio above 1.0 is generally considered good; above 2.0 is excellent; above 3.0 is exceptional and rare.

whenWhy.title: Comparing investment strategies with different risk levels
whenWhy.body: Without risk adjustment, comparing returns across strategies is misleading: a leveraged strategy earning 20% is not necessarily better than an unleveraged one earning 10% if the leveraged one is twice as volatile. The Sharpe ratio normalises for risk, enabling fair comparison. Example: Hedge Fund A earned 15% with 20% volatility (Sharpe 0.5); Hedge Fund B earned 8% with 5% volatility (Sharpe 1.0, assuming 3% risk-free rate). Fund B is better on a risk-adjusted basis despite lower absolute return. The Sharpe ratio is the slope of the Capital Market Line — it measures how efficiently risk is converted to return.

whenWhy.title: Evaluating manager skill vs. simple leverage
whenWhy.body: A manager who earns 15% by borrowing at 3% and investing 200% of capital in a 9% asset (earning 15% = 2×9% - 3%) has Sharpe = (15% - 3%) / (2 × σ_asset) = Sharpe_asset. The leverage doesn't improve the Sharpe ratio — it scales both return and risk proportionally. This is the key insight: Sharpe ratio is invariant to leverage. A manager who claims high returns through leverage without a correspondingly high Sharpe ratio is not demonstrating skill — just risk-taking. The Sharpe ratio separates alpha (skill) from beta (market exposure through leverage).

whenWhy.title: Portfolio optimisation and fund selection
whenWhy.body: The maximum Sharpe ratio portfolio is the tangency portfolio on the efficient frontier — the optimal risky portfolio that all risk-averse investors should hold (mixed with the risk-free asset according to their risk tolerance). In fund-of-funds selection, the Sharpe ratio is the primary ranking criterion. In factor investing, factors are screened partly by their long-run Sharpe ratios. The information ratio (excess return vs. benchmark / tracking error) is the Sharpe ratio analog for active management — measuring skill in generating alpha relative to active risk taken.

deepDiveIntro: The Sharpe ratio is the portfolio's average excess return (return minus the risk-free rate) divided by the standard deviation of those excess returns. The forward-looking version is used in portfolio construction to find the optimal risk-return trade-off; the historical version evaluates past performance. To annualise a Sharpe ratio computed from shorter-period returns, multiply by the square root of the number of periods per year — the square root of 12 for monthly data or the square root of 252 for daily data.

The Sharpe ratio implicitly assumes returns are normally distributed and that standard deviation fully captures risk. Strategies that earn small steady gains while carrying hidden tail risk — such as systematically selling options — can show artificially high Sharpe ratios when tail events have not yet occurred in the sample. Alternative metrics such as the Sortino ratio (which penalises only downside volatility), the Calmar ratio (return divided by maximum drawdown), and the Omega ratio address some of these blind spots.

deepDive.heading: Sharpe Ratio vs Information Ratio
deepDive.body: The Information Ratio measures active management skill: it is the active return above the benchmark divided by the tracking error. The Fundamental Law of Active Management expresses the IR as the product of the Information Coefficient — the correlation between forecasts and outcomes — and the square root of Breadth, the number of independent bets per year. A manager with modest predictive correlation who makes many independent bets can still achieve a respectable IR. This highlights why diversified active management is more efficient than concentrated management. [1]

deepDive.heading: Statistical Significance of Sharpe Ratios
deepDive.body: A high Sharpe ratio estimated from limited data may not be statistically significant. The standard error of the estimated Sharpe ratio depends on the ratio itself and the number of observations. For example, a Sharpe of 1.0 from five years of monthly data yields a 95 percent confidence interval from roughly 0.68 to 1.32 — significant but wide. A Sharpe of 0.5 from three years of monthly data is only marginally significant. A useful rule of thumb is that a track record becomes statistically meaningful when the number of observations times the squared Sharpe ratio is substantially greater than one, which typically requires at least five to ten years of data. [2]

deepDive.heading: Limitations and Alternative Risk-Adjusted Measures
deepDive.body: Sharpe ratio limitations: (1) Penalises upside volatility equally to downside — a strategy with many small gains and occasional large gains is penalised; (2) Assumes normal distribution — tail-risk strategies (short vol) have artificially high Sharpe ratios; (3) Sensitive to risk-free rate choice — different r_f assumptions produce different rankings; (4) Not comparable across investment universes without the same benchmark. Alternatives: Sortino ratio: S_sortino = (E[R_p] - r_f) / σ_downside, where σ_downside only counts returns below the risk-free rate (or target return). Calmar ratio: annualised return / maximum drawdown — relevant for managed futures and trend-following. Omega ratio: E[max(R-threshold, 0)] / E[max(threshold-R, 0)] — ratio of expected gains to expected losses; captures full return distribution, not just mean and variance. [1]

takeaways: Sharpe ratio: (E[R_p] - r_f) / σ_p — excess return per unit of total risk. [1]
Sharpe is leverage-invariant: scaling all positions doesn't change the Sharpe ratio. [1]
Statistical significance: need T × Ŝ² >> 1; 5+ year track records for meaningful evidence. [2]
Limitations: penalises upside volatility; misleading for fat-tailed return distributions. Use Sortino/Calmar for asymmetric strategies. [1]

edgeCases.q: Can the Sharpe ratio be manipulated, and how?
edgeCases.a: Yes — the Sharpe ratio can be inflated without genuine risk-adjusted performance improvement. Known manipulation techniques: (1) Return smoothing: fund NAV is marked at model prices (not market prices) for illiquid assets; stale valuations artificially reduce measured volatility; private equity and hedge funds using Level 3 assets may have inflated Sharpe ratios; (2) Picking the measurement period: report the Sharpe ratio over a period that excludes the worst drawdowns; (3) Selling options/volatility: systematically selling out-of-the-money options earns steady option premia with low measured volatility — until the tail risk realises. Sharpe ratio can be very high for a short history; (4) High-frequency sampling artifact: computing Sharpe from high-frequency data and annualising can inflate the ratio if returns have positive autocorrelation (momentum in short horizons); (5) Multiple testing / luck: with enough strategies, some will have high historical Sharpe ratios by chance alone — the minimum backtest length required grows with the number of strategies tested (Bailey & Lopez de Prado 2014: Probability of Backtest Overfitting). These issues make due diligence beyond the headline Sharpe ratio essential.

edgeCases.q: What is the optimal Sharpe ratio strategy for different investor risk tolerances?
edgeCases.a: The key insight from MVO: the highest Sharpe ratio portfolio is optimal for all investors, regardless of risk tolerance. Risk tolerance only determines the allocation between this portfolio and the risk-free asset. A conservative investor holds: 20% max Sharpe portfolio + 80% cash. An aggressive investor holds: 150% max Sharpe portfolio (borrowed) - 50% cash (leveraged). They hold the same risky portfolio in different amounts. This is the two-fund separation theorem. The practical implication: finding the maximum Sharpe portfolio (or using a diversified proxy like a multi-asset risk-parity fund) and adjusting leverage/cash allocation for risk tolerance is theoretically more efficient than constructing different portfolios for different risk tolerances. The main barriers: (1) leverage is costly for retail investors; (2) downside risk (drawdown) matters beyond standard deviation, especially for investors near retirement.

sources.label: Sharpe, W.F. (1966) — Mutual Fund Performance, Journal of Business
Lo, A.W. (2002) — The Statistics of Sharpe Ratios, Financial Analysts Journal
